title: Mörk Borg
resolution_core: d20 + ability vs DR (usually 12); players make every roll

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.d20_vs_dc: Every test is d20 plus one of four abilities (Agility, Presence, Strength, Toughness) against a Difficulty Rating, with DR12 as the assumed default and DR10-18 for easier or harder tasks.
vector_explanations.character.attributes.rolled_stats: The four abilities are rolled on 3d6 and converted to modifiers from -3 to +3, so scvm start wildly uneven and usually pathetic.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.class_based: Optional classes like Fanged Deserter, Gutterborn Scum, Esoteric Hermit, Wretched Royalty and Heretical Priest replace default dice with class-specific ability rolls, origins and special powers.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.random_back: Classless scvm are assembled from stacked random tables — Terrible Traits, Broken Bodies and Bad Habits — plus rolled silver, weapons and gear tables.
vector_explanations.resolution.luck.reroll_resources: Omens (d2 for classless scvm, larger dice for some classes) are spent to reroll a die, deal maximum damage, reduce damage taken, lower a test's DR by 4, or neutralize a crit or fumble; they refresh after rest.
vector_explanations.meta.structure_tools.countdown_timers: The Calendar of Nechrubel is a doom countdown: each dawn the GM rolls a die and on a 1 rolls d66 on the Psalms of the Nameless Scribe, inflicting a new world-scarring Misery.
vector_explanations.narrative.structure.endgame_triggers: When the seventh Misery (Psalm 7:7) comes to pass, the world is extinguished, the game formally ends, and the book instructs you to burn it.
vector_explanations.magic.acquisition.found_grimoires: Powers are learned from unclean and sacred scrolls found as loot; you can only use powers written on scrolls you physically carry.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.skill_roll_casting: Using a scroll is a Presence + d20 test versus DR12, and you can attempt powers only d4 + Presence times per day; failure leaves you dizzy and unable to cast, taking d2 damage.
vector_explanations.magic.risk.mishap_tables: A natural 1 on a power test triggers an Arcane Catastrophe rolled on its own table, spawning results like backfiring magic, demonic attention, or bodily ruin.
vector_explanations.meta.player_facing.player_facing_rolls: Monsters never roll dice: players roll Strength or Presence to attack and Agility to defend, so all combat randomness sits on the players' side of the screen.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.critical_success: A natural 20 on an attack deals double damage and degrades the target's armor by one tier; on a defense roll the defender may immediately counterattack.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.critical_failure: A natural 1 fumbles: attacking, your weapon breaks or is lost; defending, you take double damage and your own armor drops a tier.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: Hit points are Toughness plus a small class die (often d8 or less), a thin attrition buffer that keeps most scvm one bad roll from the Broken table.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.death_and_dying: At 0 HP you roll d4 on the Broken table: unconscious for d4 rounds, a severed limb or lost eye with bleeding, or simply dead — hesitation and death sit on the same chart.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.armor_as_soak: Armor subtracts a rolled die from incoming damage — light -d2, medium -d4, heavy -d6 — rather than making you harder to hit.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.armor_types: Armor comes in tiers 1-3 with explicit tradeoffs: medium armor imposes DR +2 on Agility tests and heavy armor DR +4, and crits knock armor down a tier until repaired.
vector_explanations.combat.morale.morale_checks: Enemies with a Morale score test 2d6 when half their group falls or their leader dies; exceeding Morale means they flee or surrender per the GM's roll.
vector_explanations.turns.order.rolled_initiative: A d6 decides who acts first each fight (1-3 enemies, 4-6 the party), with optional individual order from d6 + Agility.

title: Into the Odd
resolution_core: d20 roll-under ability save; attacks hit automatically

vector_explanations.character.attributes.rolled_stats: Characters roll 3d6 in order for Strength, Dexterity and Willpower plus d6 Hit Points; there are no classes, skills or bonuses beyond these numbers.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.roll_under_attr: There are no task rolls, only saves: roll d20 equal to or under the relevant ability score to avoid a danger you willingly faced.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.automatic_hit: Attacks never miss — you simply roll your weapon's damage die (d6 melee sidearm, d8 rifle, etc.) — shifting decisions from to-hit odds to positioning and whether to fight at all.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: HP is 'Hit Protection', a d6-sized buffer representing poise and luck; damage beyond it spills directly into Strength loss.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.critical_injury: When damage reduces Strength you save against the new score or suffer Critical Damage, leaving you out of action and dying within the hour without treatment.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.armor_as_soak: Armour rated 1-3 subtracts flat points from every incoming damage roll instead of altering any to-hit chance.
vector_explanations.equipment.economy_of_gear.starting_packa: Starting gear is not shopped for: you cross-reference your highest ability score with your rolled HP on the starter package table, receiving a full loadout that may include weapons, oddities or an Arcanum.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.artifact_rules: Arcana are unique reality-bending machines with individually written powers, some demanding costs or saves; hunting them is the campaign's stated engine.
vector_explanations.combat.morale.morale_checks: NPC groups make a WIL save to avoid fleeing when their leader dies or half their number falls, so most fights end before extermination.
vector_explanations.campaign.progression.milestone_advanceme: Advancement is tied to surviving play, not XP: completing your first Expedition makes you Professional, with further ranks up to Master arriving after set numbers of expeditions and seasons, each granting HP rerolls and ability improvements.
vector_explanations.downtime.activities.business_management: Established characters can found an Enterprise, investing money and rolling periodically for returns, growth and complications between expeditions.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.death_and_dying: Strength 0 means death outright; Critical Damage leaves you helpless and expiring in an hour unless an ally stabilizes you, making rescue a tactical priority.
vector_explanations.combat.restoration.short_rest: A few minutes' rest and a swig of water restores all lost Hit Protection, while lost ability scores need a week of proper care.

title: Electric Bastionland
resolution_core: d20 roll-under ability save; attacks auto-hit with Impaired/Enhanced damage dice

vector_explanations.character.character_creation.random_back: Your rolled starting money and Hit Protection index one of over a hundred two-page Failed Careers — from Bee Fanatic to Poltergeist Hunter — each granting oddball gear and a former life the game just ejected you from.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.leading_questions: Every Failed Career spread poses direct questions ('Why does the mob want you dead?') whose answers immediately become true and hook the group to its shared creditor.
vector_explanations.character.progression.no_leveling: There are no levels, XP or advances: the party collectively owes £10,000 to a debtor established at creation, and progress means treasure recovered against that debt, better gear and richer contacts.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.roll_under_attr: All uncertainty is settled by saves — d20 rolled equal to or under STR, DEX or CHA — with no attack rolls, skill lists or difficulty modifiers.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.automatic_hit: Attacks always connect and go straight to damage dice, so avoiding a fight, gaining position or attacking from safety is the real tactical layer.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: Hit Protection is a small pool of composure and luck; damage that exceeds it carves into Strength and threatens Critical Damage.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.critical_injury: Once Strength is damaged you must pass a STR save or take Critical Damage: you are out of the fight and will die unless treated, and STR 0 is death outright.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.armor_as_soak: Armour 1-3 (capped) subtracts from each damage roll; there is no evasion math, only mitigation.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.situational_modifie: Circumstance changes the die, not the roll: attacks made through cover or while vulnerable are Impaired (roll d4 damage) while unopposed or devastating attacks are Enhanced (roll d12).
vector_explanations.combat.scale.mass_combat: Detachments represent squads or mobs: individuals attacking a detachment are Impaired, detachments attacking individuals are Enhanced with blast effects, letting street riots use the same auto-hit engine.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.death_and_dying: Critical Damage leaves you dying without prompt treatment, ability loss lingers, and Strength 0 kills — the book is explicit that treasure hunts should threaten exactly this.
vector_explanations.combat.restoration.short_rest: A moment of rest and a drink restores all Hit Protection, keeping the pace brisk between scrapes while ability damage heals far more slowly.
vector_explanations.equipment.economy_of_gear.starting_packa: Each Failed Career hands you its own itemized starting equipment — often strange tools, a signature weapon, or a companion creature — with no shopping phase.

title: Cairn
resolution_core: d20 roll-under ability save; attacks auto-hit

vector_explanations.character.attributes.rolled_stats: Characters roll 3d6 for Strength, Dexterity and Willpower plus 1d6 Hit Protection, with an optional single swap between two abilities.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.random_back: Identity is stacked from random tables — name, background, physique, skin, hair, face, speech, clothing, virtue, vice, reputation and misfortune — plus rolled starting gear, spellbook or armor.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.roll_under_attr: Saves are the only roll: d20 equal to or under the relevant ability avoids danger, and anything without meaningful risk simply happens.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.automatic_hit: Attacks skip the to-hit roll entirely and go straight to the weapon die (d6 one-handed, d8 two-handed, d10 heavy), so combat is fast and genuinely dangerous.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.armor_as_soak: Armor and shields add Armor points (max 3) that subtract from every incoming damage roll rather than granting evasion.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.critical_injury: Damage beyond HP reduces Strength and forces a STR save; failure is Critical Damage, leaving the character incapacitated and dead in an hour without aid.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: Hit Protection measures composure and luck, not meat; it fully returns with brief rest, while true harm is tracked as ability damage.
vector_explanations.logistics.encumbrance.slot_based_invento: Everything you carry fills one of 10 visible inventory slots (bulky items take two), and a filled inventory drops you to 0 HP — capacity is the survival math.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.rations: Going without food, water or sleep makes you Deprived: you cannot recover HP or abilities, and extended deprivation adds Fatigue that eats inventory slots.
vector_explanations.magic.acquisition.found_grimoires: There are no spell lists by class: anyone can cast from a spellbook found in the world, each book holding a single spell and occupying an inventory slot.
vector_explanations.equipment.durability.charge_depletion: Relics hold a set number of charges (often d4-scale) with individually written recharge conditions, so magical gear is a managed resource rather than a passive bonus.
vector_explanations.exploration.procedures.reaction_rolls: When an NPC's disposition is unclear the Warden rolls 2d6 on a reaction table from hostile to helpful, so encounters are not fights by default.
vector_explanations.combat.morale.morale_checks: Enemies must pass a WIL save to avoid fleeing when their leader is slain or half their group has fallen; loners flee when their morale breaks.
vector_explanations.character.progression.no_leveling: Cairn is explicitly classless and level-less: growth is diegetic, coming from relics recovered, scars survived, spellbooks claimed and allies made rather than any XP track.
vector_explanations.combat.restoration.short_rest: A few moments of rest and a swig of water restores all lost HP, though resting is impossible while Deprived or in danger.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.death_and_dying: Strength 0 is death; Critical Damage means you are down, unable to act, and will die within the hour unless companions stabilize you.

title: Knave
resolution_core: d20 + ability bonus vs DC, or vs an opponent's static defense (bonus + 10)

vector_explanations.character.attributes.rolled_stats: For each of the six abilities you roll 3d6 and take the lowest die as your bonus, with defense always equal to bonus + 10 — a deliberately flat, swingy start.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.d20_vs_dc: Any check is d20 plus the relevant ability bonus against a Difficulty Class the GM sets, using the same six abilities for every task in the game.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.opposed_rolls: Contests skip double-rolling: the active character rolls d20 + bonus against the opponent's matching defense (their bonus + 10), so only one side ever touches dice.
vector_explanations.logistics.encumbrance.slot_based_invento: Item slots equal 10 + Constitution bonus and every item fills at least one, making Constitution the literal size of your rucksack and treasure haul.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.random_back: Knaves are fleshed out on stacked d20 tables — physique, face, speech, virtue, vice, background and misfortune — producing a distinct scoundrel in a couple of minutes.
vector_explanations.magic.acquisition.found_grimoires: Magic lives in spellbooks: each holds exactly one spell, takes an inventory slot, and can be cast once per day, so a wizard is just whoever carries the most books.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.armor_types: Armor pieces from gambeson to full plate each grant listed armor defense and quality values, trading protection against cost and inventory slots.
vector_explanations.equipment.durability.item_wear: Every item has a Quality rating: weapons lose a point of Quality on fumbled attacks and armor loses Quality when absorbing critical hits, breaking entirely at 0.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.critical_failure: A natural 1 in combat is a fumble with concrete teeth — your weapon takes Quality damage — making crit results matter beyond a miss.
vector_explanations.character.progression.experience_points: Advancement uses flat XP thresholds; at each new level a knave raises three different abilities by one point each and gains another hit die, staying classless throughout.
vector_explanations.exploration.procedures.reaction_rolls: When it is unclear how NPCs respond, a 2d6 reaction roll modified by Charisma sets their disposition, making Charisma the negotiator's stat.
vector_explanations.combat.morale.morale_checks: Monsters have a morale score checked on 2d6 when their leader falls or half the group is down; failure routs them, keeping fights short.
vector_explanations.downtime.activities.retainer_recruitment: Charisma bonus caps how many hirelings a knave can retain, and hirelings check morale when endangered or mistreated, so leadership is a stat-driven resource.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.dice_rolls: Attacks are a single d20 + Strength (melee) or Dexterity (ranged) against the target's armor defense — classic one-roll hit-or-miss resolution.

title: Troika!
resolution_core: Roll under Skill total on 2d6; opposed tests are 2d6 + Skill, high roll wins

vector_explanations.character.character_creation.random_back: A single d66 roll assigns one of 36 baroque Backgrounds — Befouler of Ponds, Claviger, Sorcerer of the Academy of Doors — each bundling Advanced Skills, possessions and spells.
vector_explanations.character.attributes.rolled_stats: Base numbers are rolled: Skill 1d3+3, Stamina 2d6+12 and Luck 1d6+6, after which your Background layers its specifics on top.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.2d6_plus_skill: Unopposed tests are Roll Under: 2d6 equal to or below Skill plus the relevant Advanced Skill succeeds, with double 6 always failing and double 1 always succeeding.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.opposed_rolls: Contests are Roll Versus: both sides roll 2d6 + Skill + Advanced Skill and the higher total wins the exchange, with ties meaning a standoff.
vector_explanations.resolution.luck.luck_stat: When pure chance decides, you Test Luck by rolling 2d6 equal to or under your current Luck — and every test reduces Luck by 1 until you rest, so fortune is a depleting stat.
vector_explanations.combat.initiative.token_bag_draw: Initiative uses a bag: each PC adds two tokens, enemies add per their entries, plus one end-of-round token; tokens are drawn one at a time, so turn order is uncertain and rounds can end abruptly.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.life_force_casting: Every spell costs Stamina — the same pool as your health — so a wizard literally spends their life to hurl Fireballs or whisper Sneezes.
vector_explanations.magic.risk.mishap_tables: Rolling a double 6 while casting triggers the Oops! table, whose d66 mishaps range from embarrassing leaks of magic to catastrophic self-destruction.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.weapon_properties_tags: Every weapon has its own row on the Damage Table — a sword and a modest club deal differently graded harm on the same d6 — with notes like ignoring armour for some arms.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.armor_as_soak: Armour steps the damage roll down by its rating (light 1, modest 2, heavy 3) before consulting the weapon's damage row, blunting rather than deflecting blows.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.rations: Provisions are itemized six at a time and each one eaten during a rest restores 1d6 Stamina, making lunch a healing resource worth fighting over.
vector_explanations.logistics.encumbrance.slot_based_invento: Possessions live on a numbered inventory list with a hard cap around a dozen items, oversized gear filling multiple lines, so what you carry is always explicit.
vector_explanations.character.skills.skill_ranks: Advanced Skills are numeric ranks added to base Skill for matching tests, and they improve through use during downtime rather than through purchased levels.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: Stamina doubles as your health pool, absorbing weapon damage after armour; it recovers through Provisions, spells and proper sleep.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.death_and_dying: There are no death saves: a character reduced to 0 Stamina is dead, which makes Provisions, healing spells and retreat genuinely strategic.

title: Heart: The City Beneath
resolution_core: d10 pool (base + skill + domain + mastery), take highest: 8-9 success, 6-7 success at cost, 10 critical

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.highest_die_poo: You roll a pool of d10s — one base, plus one each for a relevant skill, a known domain, and mastery or help — and read only the highest die against fixed outcome bands.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.success_with_cost: A highest die of 6-7 succeeds at a cost: you get what you wanted but the GM inflicts stress against one of your resistances, so most delving progress is paid for in wear.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.critical_success: A 10 is a critical success that amplifies the outcome — and on attacks it doubles the stress you inflict on the adversary's resistance.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.situational_modifie: Difficulty removes dice instead of shifting targets: Risky actions discard your highest die and Dangerous actions discard your two highest before reading the result.
vector_explanations.mental.stress.stress_track: Failed or costly rolls inflict rolled stress (d4 up to d12 by severity) against five resistances — Blood, Echo, Fortune, Mind and Supplies — tracking wounds, weirdness, luck, sanity and gear together.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.lingering_injuries: Whenever you take stress the GM rolls a d12; at or under your total stress you suffer fallout — concrete minor or major consequences like broken limbs, haunting echoes or ruined supplies that persist and clear stress.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.class_based: You pick a class such as Cleaver, Deadwalker, Deep Apostle, Junk Mage or Vermissian Knight and a calling, which together fix your core abilities, resistances and advancement menu.
vector_explanations.character.progression.zenith_abilities: Each calling culminates in Zenith abilities — apocalyptic final powers like dragging a god down with you — that resolve the character's story and remove them from play in a blaze of consequence.
vector_explanations.campaign.progression.story_xp_triggers: Advancement runs on beats: you pick minor and major beats from your calling (story events, not kill counts) and gain new abilities when they come true in play.
vector_explanations.meta.table.player_flags: Because chosen beats are visible goals like 'suffer Blood fallout' or 'find a lost settlement', they double as flags that explicitly direct the GM to aim upcoming content at each character.
vector_explanations.exploration.wilderness.depthcrawl: Delves are tracked by depth, not geography: the City Beneath descends through tiers from the surface city down to the Heart Itself, with stress dice and strangeness escalating each tier.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.clock_progress: Delves and adversaries carry resistance ratings that function as tracks: successful Delve or Kill rolls remove points equal to your stress dice until the obstacle is cleared.
vector_explanations.mental.stress.sanctuary_recovery: Stress is primarily removed at haunts — landmark refuges like bars, shrines and doctors — where you roll the haunt's die to clear stress, making safe places mapped, scarce resources.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.weapon_properties_tags: Equipment carries tags like Brutal, Bloodbound, Ranged and Degenerating that modify stress dice and impose conditions, rather than flat damage numbers.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.barter: Below the surface there is no standard coin: value moves as Resources — scavenged items with die ratings and domain tags — which are bartered, consumed and burned at haunts.

title: Spire: The City Must Fall
resolution_core: d10 pool (base + skill + domain + mastery), take highest: 8-9 success, 6-7 success at cost, 10 critical, 1 crit fail

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.highest_die_poo: Actions roll a d10 pool — one die base, plus dice for an applicable skill, domain and mastery — keeping only the highest die against fixed bands from critical failure to critical success.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.success_with_cost: The 6-7 band succeeds with a sting: the GM applies stress to one of your resistances, so revolutionary work steadily grinds the conspirators down.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.critical_success: A 10 achieves more than intended, and on violent actions doubles the stress inflicted — the system's reward for gambling on thin pools.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.critical_failure: A highest die of 1 is a critical failure that makes the situation actively worse than a mere miss, feeding the spiral of a cell operating past its limits.
vector_explanations.mental.stress.stress_track: Stress accumulates against five resistances — Blood, Mind, Silver, Shadow and Reputation — so a drow revolutionary can be broke, exposed and disgraced as easily as wounded.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.lingering_injuries: After taking stress the GM rolls a d10 against your total; rolling at or under triggers minor, moderate or severe fallout — arrests, addictions, wounds and blown safehouses that persist beyond the scene.
vector_explanations.social.influence.cover_identities: The Shadow resistance explicitly tracks your secrecy as a member of the Ministry: Shadow stress and fallout represent your revolutionary cover fraying until the aelfir authorities close in.
vector_explanations.economy.barter.abstract_wealth: Money is abstracted into the Silver resistance — financial setbacks are stress marked against it and Silver fallout means debts, destitution or seized assets rather than counted coins.
vector_explanations.social.reputation.fame_renown: Reputation is itself a resistance: standing among the drow community absorbs stress from public failures, and its fallout brands you a liability to the cause.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.class_based: You choose a durance-shaped class — Azurite, Carrion-Priest, Firebrand, Idol, Lajhan, Masked, Midwife, Vermissian Sage and more — that fixes your abilities, refresh and advancement lists.
vector_explanations.campaign.progression.milestone_advanceme: There are no XP totals: you pick low, medium or high advances from your class list when you complete missions or meaningfully change the world in the fiction.
vector_explanations.downtime.recovery.stress_relief_vice: Each class has a personal refresh condition — the Idol performs, the Firebrand stokes insurrection — and acting on it is the codified way to strip accumulated stress.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.weapon_properties_tags: Weapons and gear are defined by tags such as Accurate, Brutal, Concealable and Dangerous that alter dice and consequences instead of raw damage values.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.situational_modifie: Difficult circumstances remove your highest dice from the pool (one for Risky, two for Dangerous), so conditions bite hardest exactly when pools are small.

title: Wanderhome
resolution_core: Diceless token economy: humble trait acts earn tokens, spent for decisive acts

vector_explanations.character.character_creation.playbook_ba: Each traveler picks a distinct kith playbook — The Teacher, The Peddler, The Veteran, The Witch and others — bundling traits, questions and aesthetic pick-lists no one else at the table shares.
vector_explanations.character.identity.playbook_moves: Playbooks list things your kith 'can always' do without cost alongside trait-specific acts, so capability is written directly into who the character is.
vector_explanations.social.influence.tokens_exchange: The engine is a token economy: performing your traits' humble, vulnerable acts (giving something away, asking for help) earns tokens, which are spent to do the demanding, decisive things on your spend lists.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.shared_narration: Nobody owns the world: all players jointly describe places, weather, denizens and consequences, with the token economy rather than a referee gating dramatic swings.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.gm_less_rotation: The game explicitly supports GM-less play as a default mode, distributing facilitation across the table, with an optional single-guide variant for groups that want one.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.leading_questions: Playbooks and places constantly pose pointed questions — what you miss, what you carry, what this month means to you — whose answers immediately bind as fiction.
vector_explanations.narrative.worldbuilding.setting_question: Every place the troupe visits is built from two or three natures (Glade, Market, Graveyard and more), each supplying structured questions and detail lists that customize the world on arrival.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.seasonal_cycle_structure: Play is paced by an explicit calendar of seasons and named months, each month carrying its own tone and prompts that color the journey.
vector_explanations.downtime.activities.festivals_holidays: Seasons only turn when the troupe celebrates a holiday: the table invents the festival, performs its rituals in fiction, and answers its questions before the year moves on.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.mad_libs_prompts: Kith and places are composed from curated pick-lists — choose an animal, select traits from menus, pick look-and-feel fragments — assembling character and setting from structured phrase choices.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.questionnai: Creation is a guided sequence of in-fiction questions about who you are, why you travel and what you carry, including prompts about your fellow travelers that seed the troupe's bonds.
vector_explanations.narrative.worldbuilding.collaborative_np: The folks met on the road are created jointly in play: natures supply resident prompts and any player can introduce a denizen with wants and troubles that become canon.

title: Thousand Year Old Vampire
resolution_core: Solo journaling: d10 - d6 walks the numbered prompt list; answers are recorded as Experiences

vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.oracle_tables: Play navigates a numbered list of prompts by rolling d10 minus d6 and moving that many entries; revisiting a prompt drops you to its 'a' and 'b' sub-entries, so no two unlives take the same path.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.leading_questions: Each prompt is a pointed question — who hunts you, what have you become, what did you do to survive — answered in writing as a new Experience that becomes canon.
vector_explanations.mental.conditions.amnesia_fugues: Memory loss is the core economy: you have exactly five Memories of three Experiences each, and when they overflow you must permanently strike a Memory out, forgetting whole centuries.
vector_explanations.character.progression.journey_log: A Diary Resource can store excess Memories outside your five slots — but if the Diary is lost or destroyed by a prompt, everything written in it is gone forever.
vector_explanations.character.skills.freeform_skills: Skills are freeform phrases like 'Skulking' or 'Courtly Manners' gained from prompts and checked off when used; a checked Skill is spent until a prompt lets you recover it.
vector_explanations.economy.barter.abstract_wealth: Resources are abstract assets — a fortune, a crypt, a loyal ship — gained and lost wholesale by prompt instruction rather than tracked as currency.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.aging: Time lurches forward across prompts by years and decades, and mortal Characters must be struck out as they age and die while the vampire persists.
vector_explanations.narrative.structure.endgame_triggers: The game defines its endings: specific prompts can destroy the vampire outright, and the final prompt regions resolve what remains of you, closing the journal.
vector_explanations.character.identity.bonds_relationships: Characters — named mortals and immortals — are required mechanical objects: prompts create, entangle and kill them, and Experiences are anchored to them.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.questionnai: Setup is a guided questionnaire: you invent a mortal life, at least three Characters, several Skills and Resources, then write Experiences binding them into starting Memories before the first prompt.
vector_explanations.character.progression.permanent_scars: Marks are permanent physical signs of vampirism — a reflectionless mirror, an unhealing wound — accumulated through prompts and carried on the body forever.
vector_explanations.campaign.continuity.world_timeline_advan: The world moves by procedure: each prompt advances history, so empires fall, technologies arrive and mortal generations turn over between entries whether or not the vampire acts.

title: Lasers & Feelings
resolution_core: 1-3d6 vs your single Number (2-5): roll under for Lasers tasks, over for Feelings

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.dice_pool_succe: You roll 1d6, adding a die if you are prepared and another if you are an expert, and count how many dice beat your Number — zero to three successes map to fixed outcome bands.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.roll_under_attr: The famous twist: dice must roll under your Number for Lasers tasks (science, precision, cool logic) and over it for Feelings tasks (passion, intuition, seduction), inverting per approach.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.variable_tn: The target is personal, not situational: your single stat from 2 to 5 is simultaneously the bar for both task types, so picking 4 makes you great at Feelings and shaky at Lasers.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.degrees_of_success: Outcomes are read in named grades — 0 successes fails with GM escalation, 1 barely manages, 2 does it well, 3 is a critical with a bonus effect.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.partial_success: One success is the explicit middle band: you accomplish the task but the GM inflicts a complication, harm or cost as the price of scraping by.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.critical_success: If any die lands exactly on your Number you get Laser Feelings — a special insight letting you ask the GM a question and get an honest answer, with advantage if you act on it.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.tags_traits: Characters are two picked descriptors — a style (Alien, Android, Dangerous, Hot-Shot, Intrepid, Savvy) and a role (Doctor, Envoy, Engineer, Explorer, Soldier, Scientist) — plus a name, nothing more.
vector_explanations.character.identity.values_drives: Every character chooses a goal from a printed list (Become Captain, Meet Sexy Aliens, Prove Yourself and more) that states what they pursue during the adventure.
vector_explanations.meta.player_facing.player_facing_rolls: All dice sit with the players: threats are framed so the crew rolls to act or avoid danger while the GM adjudicates outcomes without rolling.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.situational_modifie: Circumstance changes the pool size, not the target: being prepared grants +1d and being an expert grants +1d, capping at three dice.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.oracle_tables: The GM builds the session by rolling on the Space Adventure tables — a random threat, what it wants, its target and a complication — generating the scenario in under a minute.
vector_explanations.vehicles.operation.vehicle_stats_blocks: The ship (the Raptor) is statted by group choice: the crew picks two strengths like fast, nimble, well-armed or cloaking device, and one problem like 'fuel hog' or 'grim reputation' that haunts the voyage.

title: Tales from the Loop
resolution_core: Year Zero d6 pool (attribute + skill + gear); each 6 is a success

vector_explanations.character.character_creation.class_based: Kids are built from Types — Bookworm, Computer Geek, Hick, Jock, Popular Kid, Rocker, Troublemaker, Weirdo — each packaging key skills, iconic items and example Problems and Prides.
vector_explanations.character.attributes.point_buy: Attribute points equal your Kid's age (10 to 15) distributed across Body, Tech, Heart and Mind, so older Kids are more capable but hold less Luck.
vector_explanations.character.skills.attribute_skill_pairing: Twelve skills sit three under each attribute — like Sneak under Body or Calculate under Tech — and your dice pool is always the attribute plus the paired skill.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.dice_pool_succe: You roll a d6 pool of attribute + skill (+ item bonus) and every 6 is a success; most Trouble needs one, harder feats demand two or three.
vector_explanations.luck.mitigation.luck_points_currency: Luck Points equal 15 minus age and are spent one at a time to reroll failed dice, refreshing every session — the game's mechanical case that younger Kids are luckier.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.condition_penalties: The Conditions Upset, Scared, Exhausted and Injured each impose a standing -1 die on all rolls while marked, stacking as the Mystery grinds the Kids down.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.death_and_dying: The rules state flatly that Kids cannot die: the worst outcome is being Broken, which sidelines a Kid until someone cares for them rather than killing them.
vector_explanations.downtime.recovery.condition_recovery: Conditions heal through specified remedies — chiefly spending a scene of comfort with your Anchor, the adult or friend written on your sheet who calms you down.
vector_explanations.character.identity.values_drives: Every Kid records a Drive explaining why they run toward danger, and a Pride which can be checked once per Mystery to gain an automatic success when it is truly at stake.
vector_explanations.character.identity.flaws_drawbacks: Every Kid must define a Problem — divorcing parents, a bully, unrequited love — that anchors their Everyday Life scenes and gives the GM licence to complicate the Mystery.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.help_dice: Friends who plausibly assist add +1 die each to the acting Kid's pool, encouraging the gang to face Trouble together rather than alone.
vector_explanations.character.progression.experience_points: At session end each player answers the XP questions (Did you overcome Trouble? Did your Problem affect play?) gaining a point per yes, and spends 5 XP to raise a skill one level.
vector_explanations.narrative.structure.act_breaks: Mysteries follow a codified phase structure — introducing the Kids and their everyday lives, introducing the Mystery, solving it, the Showdown, and the closing Change phase.
vector_explanations.narrative.structure.interlude_scenes: Everyday Life scenes at school and home are a distinct scene type deliberately alternated with Mystery scenes, where Problems flare and Anchors mend Conditions.

title: Kids on Bikes
resolution_core: Stat die (d4-d20) + modifiers vs difficulty; maximum rolls explode

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.step_dice: The six stats — Brains, Brawn, Fight, Flight, Charm and Grit — are each assigned one die from d4 to d20, and checks roll that stat's die against the GM's difficulty number.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.exploding_dice: Rolling a die's maximum value explodes: roll again and add, without limit, so even a d4 Brawn kid can occasionally lift the impossible.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.opposed_rolls: Contests between characters are settled by both sides rolling their relevant stat dice and comparing totals, with the higher result winning the exchange.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.bennies: Failing a roll earns an Adversity Token; tokens are spent one-for-+1 to boost rolls after seeing the result, so failure now literally banks success later.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.class_based: Characters start from Tropes — like the Brilliant Mathlete, Loner Weirdo or Washed-Up Athlete — which preassign the stat dice array and suggest strengths and flaws.
vector_explanations.character.identity.flaws_drawbacks: Every character takes defined Flaws from their Trope or the list, giving the GM levers for complications and grounding each kid or adult in believable weakness.
vector_explanations.character.progression.perks: Strengths are picked from a menu with concrete mechanical effects — such as Tough letting you shrug harm or Unassuming making adults overlook you — differentiating characters beyond stat dice.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.questionnai: Creation ends with guided questions answered with the other players about fears, motivations and shared history, wiring the group together before the mystery starts.
vector_explanations.narrative.worldbuilding.setting_question: The table collaboratively builds the small town at session start, answering structured questions about its era, name, landmarks, and local legends that become play material.
vector_explanations.actions.points.split_action_ownership: The Powered Character is collectively owned: each player holds one of its Aspects, and whoever holds the Aspect the situation invokes decides how the Powered Character acts.
vector_explanations.magic.psionics.power_points: The Powered Character fuels abilities with Psychic Energy Tokens, a depleting pool spent per use, so its reality-warping help is rationed rather than on tap.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.death_and_dying: Player characters can only die when the stakes were made explicit and the player has bought in; otherwise defeat means injury, capture or complication.

title: Fiasco
resolution_core: GM-less scene economy; a shared d6 pool seeds the Setup and drives the Tilt and Aftermath tables

vector_explanations.character.character_creation.relationshi: The Setup rolls a big central pool of d6s and players take turns claiming dice whose values buy Relationship categories and details from the playset, until every adjacent pair shares a defined relationship.
vector_explanations.character.identity.bonds_relationships: Characters have no stats at all: each one exists purely as the web of relationships, Needs, Locations and Objects attached between neighbors at the table.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.mad_libs_prompts: Playsets are books of fill-in tables — Relationships, Needs ('to get even... with the sheriff'), Locations and Objects — whose category-plus-element structure composes the story's fuses.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.scene_framing_rights: On your turn you either Establish (frame your own scene's place, people and stakes) or Resolve (the others frame it and you decide the outcome), making framing an explicitly allocated power.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.shared_narration: Whichever half you keep, the table owns the other: when you Establish, the group hands you a light or dark die mid-scene to dictate whether things go well or badly for you.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.gm_less_rotation: There is no GM at all; play rotates around the table scene by scene with the dice economy and playset tables doing the referee's job.
vector_explanations.social.influence.tilt_table: At the midpoint the players with the highest light and dark totals each pick from the Tilt table — rolled elements like 'a stupid plan, executed to perfection' — injecting escalation into Act Two.
vector_explanations.social.influence.aftermath_table: When the dice run out each player rolls their hoarded dice, subtracts the smaller color from the larger, and reads their fate on the Aftermath table from 'zero: the worst thing in the universe' upward.
vector_explanations.narrative.structure.act_breaks: Play is fixed at two acts with the Tilt between them; in Act One scene dice are given away while in Act Two you keep what you earn, structurally darkening the back half.
vector_explanations.narrative.structure.scene_economy_limits: The scene budget is hard-coded: the central pool holds four dice per player and each scene consumes exactly one, giving every player four scenes across the two acts.
vector_explanations.narrative.structure.epilogues: The Aftermath is narrated as a mandated closing montage, spent die by die in short bursts that show exactly how each character's fiasco lands.
vector_explanations.social.influence.tokens_exchange: The light and dark dice function as handed tokens: in Act One the die from your scene must be given to another player, and every die held at the end weighs your Aftermath total.

title: Microscope
resolution_core: Diceless structured turn-taking; declared Periods, Events and Scenes are canon

vector_explanations.narrative.worldbuilding.microscope_histo: The game is the trope-naming original: players build a history fractally, nesting Periods, Events and Scenes anywhere between the agreed bookend Periods, with no one owning any part of the timeline.
vector_explanations.meta.safety.content_calibration: Before play the table builds the Palette — a yes column of elements allowed even if unexpected and a no column of banned material — that binds all later contributions.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.gm_less_rotation: There is no GM: facilitation rotates through the Lens role each round, and the rules explicitly forbid collaborative brainstorming during a turn — the current player alone decides.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.fact_declaration: Whatever a player creates on their turn is immediately true; other players may not veto or contradict it, only build around it with their own additions within the Palette.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.scene_framing_rights: Whoever creates a Scene sets its Question, place and required characters, then chooses to dictate the answer outright or play the Scene out to discover it.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.leading_questions: Every played Scene must open with a stated Question — 'why did the colony burn its own fleet?' — and the Scene ends the moment play answers it.
vector_explanations.narrative.tone.tone_dials: Every Period and Event is explicitly declared Light or Dark when created, a mandatory tone marker that shapes how the history reads at a glance.
vector_explanations.meta.table.spotlight_rotation: The Lens rotates each round: that player declares the Focus everyone's contributions must involve, guaranteeing each person periodically steers the history's attention.
vector_explanations.turns.order.fixed_clockwise: Within a round play passes in fixed seating order, each player making exactly one move — a new Period, Event or Scene — before the Lens closes the round.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.shared_narration: Authority over the world is fully distributed: any player may nest new Periods or Events anywhere in the timeline on their turn, so the setting has no single author.
vector_explanations.narrative.worldbuilding.collaborative_np: In played Scenes the group divides up whatever characters the moment requires, inventing them on the spot, with required characters assignable by the scene's creator.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Rounds have a fixed procedure: the Lens declares a Focus, players each take a turn (the Lens takes two, first and last), then a Legacy phase revisits an enduring element before the Lens passes on.
